---
editor:
    render-on-save: true
csl: ieee.csl
format:
  html:
    filters: [tikz.lua, strip-yaml-frontmatter.lua, maintext-filter.lua, sitext-filter.lua]
---

## Compositional Robustness in the LNM and PLN Models {#sec-diffexp-lnm-pln-robust}

The Dirichlet-Multinomial framework enjoys a strong compositional robustness
guarantee: the inferred per-cell composition $\underline{\rho}^{(c)}$ is
*exactly* invariant under misspecification of the per-cell capture
probability $\nu_c$ (see @sec-diffexp-robustness). The result rests on a
single structural property of the DM generative model — binomial thinning
acts only on the rate parameter of the latent Gamma, leaving the shape
parameter $\underline{r}$ (and hence the Dirichlet conditional) untouched.

The Logistic-Normal Multinomial (LNM) and Poisson-LogNormal (PLN) models
introduced in @sec-lnm-logistic-normal-def and @sec-pln-distribution were
designed for a different purpose — capturing gene-gene correlations that the
DM cannot represent — but a natural follow-up question is whether the same
robustness guarantee carries over. The answer depends on which model:

* **LNM(VCP) (@sec-diffexp-lnm-pln-robust-lnm)**: The composition is
  exactly invariant to capture misspecification, *by construction*. The
  argument is structurally identical to the DM proof, with the multinomial
  conditional on the simplex $\text{ALR}^{-1}(\underline{y}_{\text{ALR}})$
  playing the role that the Dirichlet conditional plays in the DM.
* **PLN (@sec-diffexp-lnm-pln-robust-pln)**: The composition is invariant
  *up to a global log-shift*. Compositional contrasts (log-ratios) and the
  centred-log-ratio (CLR) representation are invariant, but the absolute
  log-rates $\underline{x}^{(c)}$ absorb the capture-scaling error. PLN
  inherits the robustness theorem in its **Aitchison-geometry form**, but
  not in its absolute-rate form.

Both statements are conceptually familiar — they are the model-specific
translations of the unidentifiability result $\text{Multinomial}(u_T, \rho)$
that motivated the biology-informed capture anchor of @sec-capture-prior.
What this section adds is the formal derivation in each model's own
parameterization, plus a diagnostic interpretation of the *visual*
signature of the unidentifiability (a cleanly linear $\hat{p}_{\text{capture}}^{(c)}$
versus $L_c$ plot) that we have observed in real fits.

### Setup and notation {#sec-diffexp-lnm-pln-robust-setup}

We work throughout with the variable-capture generative process introduced
in @sec-lnm-totals-parameterizations and @sec-pln-capture. For each cell $c$ with observed
library size $L_c = \sum_g u_g^{(c)}$:

* Per-cell **capture probability** $p_{\text{capture}}^{(c)} \in (0, 1]$,
  modelled with the biology-informed log-normal anchor
  $\eta^{(c)} \sim \mathcal{N}(\log M_0 - \log L_c,\, \sigma_M^2)$ and
  $p_{\text{capture}}^{(c)} = e^{-\eta^{(c)}}$, where $M_0$ is the prior
  median total mRNA per cell.
* **True (pre-capture) total mRNA** mean parameter $\mu_T$ shared across
  cells; the per-cell expected total satisfies $\langle u_T^{(c)} \rangle
  = \mu_T \cdot p_{\text{capture}}^{(c)}$.
* **Misspecification ratio** $\kappa = p_{\text{capture}}^{(c)} /
  \tilde{p}_{\text{capture}}^{(c)}$, where $\tilde{p}_{\text{capture}}^{(c)}$
  is the value the inferred fit lands on. We treat $\kappa$ as the
  per-cell quantity that any robustness statement must absorb.

The data quantity that is unambiguously identified is the *expected
observed total*

$$
\langle u_T^{(c)} \rangle \;=\; \mu_T \cdot p_{\text{capture}}^{(c)}
\;=\; \tilde{\mu}_T \cdot \tilde{p}_{\text{capture}}^{(c)},
$${#eq-diffexp-lnmpln-data-product}

so any consistent fit lies on the one-dimensional ridge $\mu_T \cdot
p_{\text{capture}}^{(c)} = \text{const}$. The biology-informed prior on
$\eta^{(c)}$ (equivalently on $p_{\text{capture}}^{(c)}$) breaks this
non-identifiability *probabilistically* but does not move the data-induced
ridge.

The robustness question is then: when the model picks the "wrong" point on
the ridge — say $\tilde{\mu}_T = \kappa \cdot \mu_T$ paired with
$\tilde{p}_{\text{capture}}^{(c)} = p_{\text{capture}}^{(c)} / \kappa$ —
which downstream quantities change, and which do not?

### LNM(VCP): composition is exactly invariant {#sec-diffexp-lnm-pln-robust-lnm}

The LNM generative model factorizes the per-cell observation into two
conditionally independent submodels (see @sec-lnm-totals-parameterizations and
@sec-lnm-laplace-full-model):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(totals submodel)} \quad &
u_T^{(c)} \;\sim\; \mathrm{NB}\!\left(r_T,\, \mu_T \cdot p_{\text{capture}}^{(c)}\right), \\
\text{(composition submodel)} \quad &
\underline{u}^{(c)} \mid u_T^{(c)},\, \underline{y}_{\text{ALR}}^{(c)}
\;\sim\; \mathrm{Multinomial}\!\left(u_T^{(c)},\, \text{ALR}^{-1}(\underline{y}_{\text{ALR}}^{(c)})\right),
\end{aligned}
$${#eq-diffexp-lnmpln-lnm-factorization}

with the compositional latent
$\underline{y}_{\text{ALR}}^{(c)} \sim \mathcal{N}(\underline{\mu},\,
\underline{\underline{W}}\,\underline{\underline{W}}^\top + \mathrm{diag}(\underline{d}))$
unchanged across cells. The two submodels share *no* random variables: the
capture probability $p_{\text{capture}}^{(c)}$ enters the joint density
only through the totals submodel.

#### Theorem (LNM compositional invariance) {#sec-diffexp-lnmpln-lnm-theorem}

Let $\mathcal{L}(\underline{\theta})$ denote the joint log-likelihood
of the LNM(VCP) generative model with global parameters
$\underline{\theta} = \{\underline{\mu},\, \underline{\underline{W}},\,
\underline{d},\, \mu_T,\, r_T,\, \{p_{\text{capture}}^{(c)}\}_{c=1}^C\}$.
For any per-cell scaling $\kappa \in (0, \infty)$, the reparameterization

$$
\tilde{\mu}_T = \kappa \cdot \mu_T, \qquad
\tilde{p}_{\text{capture}}^{(c)} = p_{\text{capture}}^{(c)} / \kappa,
$${#eq-diffexp-lnmpln-lnm-reparam}

leaves the data-likelihood term invariant:
$\mathcal{L}_{\text{data}}(\underline{\tilde{\theta}}) =
\mathcal{L}_{\text{data}}(\underline{\theta})$. Furthermore, the
posterior over the compositional latent
$q(\underline{y}_{\text{ALR}}^{(c)})$ is **identical** under
$\underline{\theta}$ and $\underline{\tilde{\theta}}$.

#### Proof {#sec-diffexp-lnmpln-lnm-proof}

The data-likelihood factorizes as

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{data}}(\underline{\theta}) \;=\;
\sum_{c=1}^{C} \log \mathrm{NB}\!\left(u_T^{(c)} \,\big|\, r_T,\, \mu_T \cdot p_{\text{capture}}^{(c)}\right)
+ \sum_{c=1}^{C} \log \mathrm{Multinomial}\!\left(\underline{u}^{(c)} \,\big|\, u_T^{(c)},\, \text{ALR}^{-1}(\underline{y}_{\text{ALR}}^{(c)})\right).
$${#eq-diffexp-lnmpln-lnm-loglik}

Under @eq-diffexp-lnmpln-lnm-reparam, the NB mean parameter is unchanged:

$$
\tilde{\mu}_T \cdot \tilde{p}_{\text{capture}}^{(c)}
\;=\; (\kappa \cdot \mu_T) \cdot (p_{\text{capture}}^{(c)} / \kappa)
\;=\; \mu_T \cdot p_{\text{capture}}^{(c)}.
$${#eq-diffexp-lnmpln-lnm-nb-invariant}

The NB log-density at $u_T^{(c)}$ depends on the global parameters only
through this product (and through $r_T$, which is unchanged). The first
sum in @eq-diffexp-lnmpln-lnm-loglik is therefore invariant. The second
sum depends on $\underline{u}^{(c)}$, $u_T^{(c)}$, and
$\underline{y}_{\text{ALR}}^{(c)}$ but **not** on $\mu_T$ or
$p_{\text{capture}}^{(c)}$ — capture and scaling do not appear in the
multinomial likelihood. The full data-likelihood is therefore invariant.

For the second claim, observe that the marginal posterior over
$\underline{y}_{\text{ALR}}^{(c)}$ is

$$
q(\underline{y}_{\text{ALR}}^{(c)} \mid \underline{u}^{(c)}, u_T^{(c)})
\;\propto\;
\mathrm{Multinomial}(\underline{u}^{(c)} \mid u_T^{(c)},\, \text{ALR}^{-1}(\underline{y}_{\text{ALR}}^{(c)}))
\,\cdot\,
\mathcal{N}(\underline{y}_{\text{ALR}}^{(c)} \mid \underline{\mu},\, \underline{\underline{\Sigma}}),
$${#eq-diffexp-lnmpln-lnm-y-posterior}

with $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}} = \underline{\underline{W}}\,
\underline{\underline{W}}^\top + \mathrm{diag}(\underline{d})$. Neither
factor depends on $\mu_T$ or $p_{\text{capture}}^{(c)}$, so the entire
posterior is identical under $\underline{\theta}$ and
$\underline{\tilde{\theta}}$. $\blacksquare$

#### Consequences for differential expression {#sec-diffexp-lnmpln-lnm-de}

Under any compositional contrast — pairwise log-ratio, full ALR, or CLR —
the test statistic is a function of $\underline{y}_{\text{ALR}}^{(c)}$
alone. The theorem states that this latent has identical posterior under
any $\kappa$-shifted scaling of the totals submodel. Therefore:

> **The LNM compositional inference is exactly invariant to capture
> probability misspecification, in precisely the same sense as the DM**
> (@eq-diffexp-rob-dirichlet-invariant). All gene-level differential
> expression test statistics built from $\underline{y}_{\text{ALR}}^{(c)}$
> are robust to misspecification of $M_0$ or $\sigma_M$.

The only quantity that can absorb capture-scaling error is the totals
submodel itself, and the absorption is one-dimensional: only the product
$\mu_T \cdot p_{\text{capture}}^{(c)}$ is identified, so any reported
absolute $\hat{\mu}_T$ should be interpreted as the data-likelihood
solution at the prior-pinned $\hat{p}_{\text{capture}}^{(c)}$ point on the
ridge.

### PLN: invariance restricted to compositional contrasts {#sec-diffexp-lnm-pln-robust-pln}

The PLN model has no separate totals submodel. Capture enters as an
additive scalar offset in log-rate space (@sec-pln-capture):

$$
u_g^{(c)} \mid x_g^{(c)},\, \eta^{(c)} \;\sim\; \mathrm{Poisson}\!\left(e^{x_g^{(c)} - \eta^{(c)}}\right),
\qquad
\underline{x}^{(c)} \;\sim\; \mathcal{N}(\underline{\mu},\, \underline{\underline{\Sigma}}),
$${#eq-diffexp-lnmpln-pln-generative}

with $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}} = \underline{\underline{W}}\,
\underline{\underline{W}}^\top + \mathrm{diag}(\underline{d})$. The
capture offset is a scalar, so it shifts every gene's log-rate by the
same amount. Compositions and absolute rates therefore behave
asymmetrically.

#### Theorem (PLN log-ratio invariance) {#sec-diffexp-lnmpln-pln-theorem}

For any global shift $\Delta \in \mathbb{R}$, the joint log-likelihood
$\mathcal{L}(\underline{\theta})$ of the PLN model is invariant under
the reparameterization

$$
\tilde{\mu}_g = \mu_g + \Delta \;\;\forall g, \qquad
\tilde{\eta}^{(c)} = \eta^{(c)} + \Delta \;\;\forall c.
$${#eq-diffexp-lnmpln-pln-translation}

Equivalently: the model has a one-parameter rigid-translation degeneracy
between $\underline{\mu}$ and the per-cell offsets $\{\eta^{(c)}\}$.
Under this degeneracy, the centred log-rate vector

$$
\underline{\tilde{x}}^{(c)} \;\equiv\; \underline{x}^{(c)} - \bar{x}^{(c)} \mathbf{1},
\quad \text{with } \bar{x}^{(c)} = \tfrac{1}{G} \sum_{g=1}^G x_g^{(c)},
$${#eq-diffexp-lnmpln-pln-clr}

and any pairwise log-ratio $x_g^{(c)} - x_h^{(c)}$ have identical
posterior distribution under $\underline{\theta}$ and
$\underline{\tilde{\theta}}$.

#### Proof {#sec-diffexp-lnmpln-pln-proof}

The Poisson rate appearing in the likelihood is

$$
e^{x_g^{(c)} - \eta^{(c)}}.
$${#eq-diffexp-lnmpln-pln-rate}

Under the prior $\underline{x}^{(c)} \sim \mathcal{N}(\underline{\mu},
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}})$, define the shifted latent
$\underline{x}'^{(c)} = \underline{x}^{(c)} + \Delta \mathbf{1}$. Then
$\underline{x}'^{(c)} \sim \mathcal{N}(\underline{\mu} + \Delta \mathbf{1},
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}})$ — note that the covariance is
unchanged because adding a constant to each coordinate is a translation,
not a rotation, of the latent space. The Poisson rate becomes

$$
e^{x_g'^{(c)} - \tilde{\eta}^{(c)}}
\;=\; e^{x_g^{(c)} + \Delta - (\eta^{(c)} + \Delta)}
\;=\; e^{x_g^{(c)} - \eta^{(c)}},
$${#eq-diffexp-lnmpln-pln-rate-invariant}

i.e., the per-gene Poisson rate is unchanged by the joint shift. Both
the data-likelihood and the latent prior contribute identically to the
joint log-density under $\underline{\theta}$ and
$\underline{\tilde{\theta}}$, establishing the first claim.

For the second claim, observe that the centred log-rate satisfies

$$
\tilde{x}_g^{(c)} \;=\; x_g^{(c)} - \tfrac{1}{G}\sum_{g'} x_{g'}^{(c)},
\quad \tilde{x}_g'^{(c)} \;=\; \tilde{x}_g^{(c)} + \Delta - \Delta \;=\; \tilde{x}_g^{(c)},
$${#eq-diffexp-lnmpln-pln-clr-invariant}

so $\underline{\tilde{x}}^{(c)}$ is a deterministic function of
$\underline{x}^{(c)}$ that is invariant to the rigid translation. The
posterior distribution of any function of
$\underline{\tilde{x}}^{(c)}$ is therefore identical under
$\underline{\theta}$ and $\underline{\tilde{\theta}}$. The pairwise
log-ratio $x_g^{(c)} - x_h^{(c)}$ is one such function. $\blacksquare$

#### Where the degeneracy lives in the PLN {#sec-diffexp-lnmpln-pln-degeneracy}

The PLN $(\underline{\mu}, \eta^{(c)})$ degeneracy is the structural
analogue of the LNM $(\mu_T, p_{\text{capture}}^{(c)})$ scaling
degeneracy: in both cases, a one-parameter family of parameter values
produces identical data-likelihood, and in both cases the prior on the
capture quantity is what selects a point on the family. The difference
is *which directions* in parameter space are degenerate:

| Model | Capture coupling | Degeneracy direction | Prior breaks degeneracy via |
|-------|------------------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| LNM(VCP) | $\langle u_T^{(c)} \rangle = \mu_T \cdot p_{\text{capture}}^{(c)}$ (multiplicative on totals) | $(\mu_T, p_{\text{capture}}^{(c)}) \to (\kappa \mu_T, p_{\text{capture}}^{(c)}/\kappa)$ | log-normal anchor on $\eta^{(c)} = -\log p_{\text{capture}}^{(c)}$ |
| PLN | $\lambda_g^{(c)} = e^{x_g^{(c)} - \eta^{(c)}}$ (additive on log-rates) | $(\mu_g, \eta^{(c)}) \to (\mu_g + \Delta, \eta^{(c)} + \Delta)$ | log-normal anchor on $\eta^{(c)}$ |

In both rows the prior on $\eta^{(c)}$ does the same job: it selects a
specific point on the data-induced ridge. In LNM the ridge is
*multiplicative* in scale (a single scalar $\kappa$ per cell), while in
PLN it is *additive* in log-rate (a single scalar $\Delta$ shared across
*all* cells through $\underline{\mu}$). The PLN degeneracy is therefore
*more restrictive* than the LNM one — it ties all cells together via
the population mean — but it has the same projection-onto-contrasts
structure.

#### Consequences for differential expression {#sec-diffexp-lnmpln-pln-de}

The robustness theorem holds for any test statistic that is a function
of the centred log-rate $\underline{\tilde{x}}^{(c)}$ or any pairwise
log-ratio $x_g^{(c)} - x_h^{(c)}$. Concretely:

* **CLR-based DE** is robust. Reporting per-gene effects as
  $\tilde{x}_g^{(\text{cond}_A)} - \tilde{x}_g^{(\text{cond}_B)}$ or as
  the contrast $(x_g - x_h)^{(\text{cond}_A)} - (x_g - x_h)^{(\text{cond}_B)}$
  inherits the robustness theorem identically.
* **Absolute log-fold change of $\mu_g$** is *not* robust. Any reported
  $\mu_g^{(\text{cond}_A)} - \mu_g^{(\text{cond}_B)}$ absorbs the
  per-condition $\Delta$ shift if the conditions have different capture
  scaling. This is the PLN-specific failure mode that does not arise in
  LNM (where $\underline{\mu}$ lives in ALR space and is structurally
  centred).
* **Absolute total mRNA per cell** is not robust in either model: in
  LNM it absorbs $\kappa$, in PLN it absorbs $e^{\Delta}$.

The recommended workflow for capture-robust DE in the PLN family is
therefore to report effects in CLR space (or equivalently, in pairwise
log-ratios), matching the recommended workflow in the DM and LNM
families. We discuss the practical mechanics of this in
@sec-diffexp-lnmpln-recommendations below.

### Comparison across model families {#sec-diffexp-lnm-pln-robust-comparison}

The three model families share a common structural pattern: each has a
one-parameter unidentifiability between a *scale* parameter and a
per-cell *capture* parameter, and each protects a specific class of
*compositional* downstream quantities from that unidentifiability. The
formal symmetry is summarized in the following table:

| Family | Composition latent | Capture mechanism | Class of robust quantities |
|--------|--------------------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| DM | $\underline{\rho}^{(c)} \sim \mathrm{Dirichlet}(\underline{r})$ | binomial thinning $\mathrm{Binomial}(m_g, \nu_c)$ | full simplex $\underline{\rho}^{(c)}$ (@eq-diffexp-rob-dirichlet-invariant) |
| LNM(VCP) | $\underline{y}_{\text{ALR}}^{(c)} \sim \mathcal{N}(\underline{\mu},\, \underline{\underline{\Sigma}})$ | NB-on-totals scaling $\mu_T \cdot p_{\text{capture}}^{(c)}$ | full ALR latent $\underline{y}_{\text{ALR}}^{(c)}$ (@sec-diffexp-lnmpln-lnm-theorem) |
| PLN | $\underline{x}^{(c)} \sim \mathcal{N}(\underline{\mu},\, \underline{\underline{\Sigma}})$ in absolute log-rates | scalar log-rate offset $-\eta^{(c)} \mathbf{1}$ | centred / pairwise contrasts $\underline{\tilde{x}}^{(c)}$ (@sec-diffexp-lnmpln-pln-theorem) |

The asymmetry is informative. DM and LNM both protect a *full
$(G-1)$-dimensional* compositional object — the entire simplex point —
because both have a composition latent that lives natively on (or in
1-1 correspondence with) the simplex. PLN has only *absolute* log-rates
as primary latents, and the simplex emerges only as the projection
$\underline{\tilde{x}}^{(c)}$ that quotients out the rigid-translation
degeneracy. This is consistent with the broader statement made in
@sec-pln-contrast: the LNM and PLN are equivalent on the simplex but
differ in what they identify off it.

### Diagnostic: the linear $\hat{p}_{\text{capture}}^{(c)}$ vs $L_c$ plot {#sec-diffexp-lnm-pln-robust-diagnostic}

A useful empirical diagnostic for the data-induced ridge is to plot the
fitted $\hat{p}_{\text{capture}}^{(c)}$ against the observed library
size $L_c$. Under the LNMVCP joint MAP, the data-likelihood drives
$\hat{\eta}^{(c)} \approx \log \hat{\mu}_T - \log L_c$ (since the
likelihood has only one knob per cell), so

$$
\hat{p}_{\text{capture}}^{(c)} \;=\; e^{-\hat{\eta}^{(c)}}
\;\approx\; \frac{L_c}{\hat{\mu}_T}.
$${#eq-diffexp-lnmpln-pcap-line}

Equivalently, the fit produces a perfectly linear plot of
$\hat{p}_{\text{capture}}^{(c)}$ against $L_c$ with slope $1/\hat{\mu}_T$.
The slope is therefore a direct read-out of the model's posterior on the
total mRNA scale parameter. If the prior on $\eta^{(c)}$ were strong
enough to dominate the per-cell likelihood, the plot would deviate from
the line; in practice, the NB-on-totals likelihood with $C \sim 10^3$
cells of UMI depth $\sim 10^4$ is overwhelmingly informative compared to
any reasonable $\sigma_M$, and the line is essentially exact.

This is the visual signature of the unidentifiability documented in
@eq-diffexp-lnmpln-data-product: the model has parked itself on a single
point on the data-likelihood ridge, with the prior acting as a tiebreaker
along the ridge but not pinning the absolute scale. The compositional
robustness theorem states that this is *not a problem* for any
downstream compositional inference; it would only be a problem if one
tried to interpret $\hat{\mu}_T$ as the absolute total mRNA per cell
without acknowledging the prior's role in selecting that value.

### Practical recommendations {#sec-diffexp-lnmpln-recommendations}

The robustness theorems above translate into a small set of practical
rules for using the LNM and PLN models in differential expression
analysis:

1. **In LNM(VCP)**: report effects on the ALR latent
   $\underline{y}_{\text{ALR}}^{(c)}$, on the simplex
   $\underline{\rho}^{(c)} = \text{ALR}^{-1}(\underline{y}_{\text{ALR}}^{(c)})$,
   or on log-fold-changes of these quantities across conditions. The
   robustness theorem then guarantees that misspecification of $M_0$ or
   $\sigma_M$ leaves the inference unchanged. Do not interpret
   $\hat{\mu}_T$ as the absolute total mRNA per cell without the caveat
   that it is co-determined with $\hat{p}_{\text{capture}}^{(c)}$ along
   a one-parameter ridge.
2. **In PLN**: report effects on the centred log-rate
   $\underline{\tilde{x}}^{(c)} = \underline{x}^{(c)} - \bar{x}^{(c)} \mathbf{1}$
   or on pairwise log-ratios $x_g^{(c)} - x_h^{(c)}$. Avoid reporting
   absolute log-fold-changes of $\hat{\mu}_g$, particularly when
   comparing conditions that may have different per-cell capture
   distributions.
3. **In both families**: tighten the prior $\sigma_M$ if the absolute
   $\hat{\mu}_T$ (LNM) or $\hat{\mu}_g$ (PLN) is needed for downstream
   interpretation, since these quantities sit on the unidentifiable
   ridge and are pinned only by the prior.
4. **Cross-model validation**: a useful sanity check is to verify that
   recomputing the same DE statistic under $\hat{\mu}_T \to \kappa
   \hat{\mu}_T$, $\hat{p}_{\text{capture}}^{(c)} \to
   \hat{p}_{\text{capture}}^{(c)} / \kappa$ (LNM) or
   $\hat{\mu}_g \to \hat{\mu}_g + \Delta$, $\hat{\eta}^{(c)} \to
   \hat{\eta}^{(c)} + \Delta$ (PLN) leaves the DE conclusions unchanged.
   The theorems above guarantee this analytically; verifying it
   numerically gives a useful consistency check on the implementation.

### Summary {#sec-diffexp-lnm-pln-robust-summary}

The Dirichlet-Multinomial robustness theorem (@eq-diffexp-rob-dirichlet-invariant)
generalizes to both LNM and PLN, but with a model-specific projection of
the protected quantity:

* **LNM(VCP)** protects the *full* compositional latent
  $\underline{y}_{\text{ALR}}^{(c)}$ — directly analogous to the DM
  protecting $\underline{\rho}^{(c)}$.
* **PLN** protects the *centred* log-rate
  $\underline{\tilde{x}}^{(c)}$ and any function thereof (pairwise
  log-ratios, CLR statistics) — but *not* the absolute log-rates.

In all three families, the unidentifiability is a one-parameter
direction in parameter space, and in all three families the
biology-informed capture prior selects a specific point on the ridge
while the *compositional* downstream inferences are invariant to that
selection. This common structural pattern explains why all three models
work well for compositional DE despite their different generative
mechanics, and clarifies which absolute quantities should be interpreted
with caution.
